critic_raw:
The methodology is well-structured with genuine attention to the most common failure modes in educational data mining (temporal leakage, class imbalance, distributional consistency checks). However, I identify three issues worth pressing on, organized by root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: The comparison conflates model class and feature representation**

The specific claim: the experiment isolates 'the contribution of sequence information and gradient boosting' by comparing XGBoost-on-sequences vs. logistic-regression-on-aggregates.

Why it might be wrong: The design confounds two independent variables simultaneously — the feature representation (raw sequences vs. aggregated statistics) and the model class (gradient-boosted trees vs. logistic regression). A gradient-boosted model trained on the same aggregated features would almost certainly outperform logistic regression on those same features, simply because GBDT can capture nonlinear relationships and feature interactions that logistic regression cannot. If the sequence model wins, you cannot tell whether the win comes from sequence structure or from the nonlinear model class. The stated isolation goal is not achieved by this design.

What would constitute evidence: The critical missing condition is GBDT trained on aggregated features. If GBDT-on-aggregates matches or beats GBDT-on-sequences, sequences add no value. If GBDT-on-sequences beats GBDT-on-aggregates, sequence structure contributes above and beyond model expressiveness. The current design cannot distinguish these cases.

---

**Root Cause 2: The sequence representation for GBDT is likely degenerate**

The specific claim: a gradient-boosted decision tree can exploit temporal ordering information from a zero-padded, fixed-length (500×4) event matrix.

Why it might be wrong: Gradient-boosted decision trees operate on flat feature vectors. When the 500×4 matrix is flattened into a 2,000-dimensional input, each tree split is on a single positional feature — e.g., 'the event_type value at position 237 in the padded sequence.' This is fundamentally position-dependent, not order-dependent in any meaningful sense. Two learners with the same 50 events but different padding lengths will have those events at completely different positions in the flat vector, and the model has no mechanism to treat them equivalently. The result is that the model is likely learning from early-sequence density (non-padded positions), late-sequence absence (zero-padded positions), and possibly spurious positional correlations — not temporal patterns. The aggregated baseline may already capture most real signal, and the sequence model's apparent advantage (if any) could be an artifact of the implicit 'total event count' signal embedded in the padding boundary position.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Compare the sequence model against GBDT-on-aggregates (as above) to see if there's any residual gain. (b) Examine which feature positions the GBDT relies on most (SHAP values) — heavy reliance on late padded positions would confirm this artifact. (c) Alternatively, replacing the padded sequence input with summary statistics computed from the sequence (mean/std/autocorrelation of inter-event times, etc.) would constitute a fairer test of whether temporal structure matters, because that representation is stable to sequence length variation.

---

**Root Cause 3: The dropout label definition creates a survivorship asymmetry that may not be visible in the distributional consistency check**

The specific claim: the ±5 percentage point dropout rate check across cohorts confirms distributional consistency sufficient for generalization.

Why it might be wrong: The dropout label depends on observing 7 consecutive inactive days 'at any point after day 8.' For learners in early cohorts (months 1–12), the platform has had 12–24 months to observe this window — all true dropouts have had time to manifest. For learners in the test cohort (months 16–24), some learners enrolled in months 22–24 may not have had 7 post-week-1 days of follow-up by the time the data snapshot was taken, meaning recent enrollees are disproportionately labeled as non-dropouts by data truncation, not by actual retention. This artificially deflates the dropout rate in the test cohort's tail. The ±5pp check will catch gross drift but will not detect this specific truncation artifact, because the rate stays roughly consistent in the early part of the test window and diverges only in the most recent enrollment weeks. The model trained on complete-observation cohorts may then appear to generalize well on most of the test set while being systematically miscalibrated on the most recent (and operationally most relevant) learners.

What would constitute evidence: Report dropout label completeness as a function of enrollment date — specifically, the fraction of learners for whom the post-week-1 observation window is fully observed (≥7 days after day 8). Restrict evaluation to learners with complete follow-up, or apply a survival-analysis formulation. If performance degrades monotonically in the most recent enrollment weeks of the test cohort, truncation is the likely cause.
all_issues_raised: The baseline comparison conflates model class (linear vs. GBDT) with feature representation (aggregated vs. sequential), making it impossible to attribute any performance gain to sequence information specifically.

Flattening a variable-length padded event sequence into a fixed-length vector for a GBDT means splits are position-dependent, not order-dependent, so the model likely cannot exploit temporal structure and may instead learn from padding boundary artifacts.

The dropout label requires observing 7 consecutive inactive days after day 8, but learners enrolled late in the test cohort may be truncated before this window closes, biasing their labels toward non-dropout and creating a miscalibration artifact not detectable by the stated distributional consistency check.
all_issues_adjudicated: The baseline comparison conflates model class (linear vs. GBDT) with feature representation (aggregated vs. sequential), making it impossible to attribute any performance gain to sequence information specifically.

Flattening a variable-length padded event sequence into a fixed-length vector for a GBDT means splits are position-dependent, not order-dependent, so the model likely cannot exploit temporal structure and may instead learn from padding boundary artifacts.

The dropout label requires observing 7 consecutive inactive days after day 8, but learners enrolled late in the test cohort may be truncated before this window closes, biasing their labels toward non-dropout and creating a miscalibration artifact not detectable by the stated distributional consistency check.
